segments.title: Sea Flight and the Fleet Air Arm's global war
segments.summary: Sea Flight uses one pilot's wartime recollections to show how Fleet Air Arm squadrons escorted convoys and fought from carriers around the world.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends Sea Flight as a personal account of a Hurricane pilot serving from HMS Indomitable, moving between the Atlantic, West Indies, Ceylon and Madagascar. The excerpt contrasts routine convoy protection with low-level attacks under fire, including an operation in which naval aircraft and Royal Marines helped break a shore-side stalemate. The recommendation makes the memoir a way into the geographically dispersed, dangerous work of carrier aviation during the Second World War.

evidence.note: The review describes a Fleet Air Arm memoir, its convoy-patrol setting, a combat episode, and its contents.

segments.title: Ark Royal's career, design debate and wreck record
segments.summary: A history of HMS Ark Royal follows the carrier from development to loss while opening questions about how her design should be interpreted.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke recommends Mike Rossiter's Ark Royal as a full career history, covering the carrier's operations and voyages from beginning to end. He values its pictures and presentation while cautioning that he does not wholly share its standardized account of the ship's development and design. The underwater images of Swordfish and Fulmar wreckage also connect the book to the later effort to locate and understand Ark Royal's remains.

evidence.note: The review assesses the Ark Royal history, its design treatment, and photographs connected with the wreck search.

segments.title: Swordfish, Taranto and carrier-landing culture
segments.summary: A Swordfish history of the Taranto raid brings carrier aviation's operational effect together with the humour and hazards of wartime deck landings.
segments.body: Dr. Clarke praises David Wragg's Swordfish history of the Taranto raid for its exceptional images and for presenting quotations from both sides of the conflict. A dark Fleet Air Arm joke about whether a pilot can swim conveys why a bad carrier landing could be fatal. He also highlights Cunningham's barbed recollection of a pre-war visit with Admiral Riccardi, placing naval personality beside the later Italian campaign context.

evidence.note: The review discusses the Swordfish book, its Taranto subject, a Fleet Air Arm landing joke, and the Cunningham-Riccardi anecdote.

segments.title: Naval Wings: a Swordfish pilot's wider war
segments.summary: Naval Wings follows a Fleet Air Arm Swordfish pilot from Mediterranean strikes to the Atlantic and the war against Japan.
segments.body: The review introduces John Wellham's Naval Wings through his service with 824 Squadron aboard HMS Eagle and the Swordfish operations that preceded the Taranto raid. Its scope then extends through Atlantic and anti-submarine work to the final campaign against Japan, showing how one aviator's career crossed several theatres. Wellham presents the memoir as a personal record supported by logbooks, photographs and official checks, while acknowledging that memory and records do not always agree.

evidence.note: The review outlines Wellham's Swordfish service, the memoir's theatres, and its account of personal memory checked against records.
